name: Classical Wiener Process with Per-Action Accumulators
description: Implements the canonical drift-diffusion model (Ratcliff, 1978) as a discrete-time Wiener process with independent parallel accumulators, one per candidate action. Each accumulator starts at a common starting point z0 and accumulates evidence at a drift rate computed from perceptual proximity to resources and past reward history, plus Gaussian noise. The first accumulator to cross the fixed upper boundary 'a' determines the chosen action (race model). If no accumulator crosses by T_max steps, the action with the highest accumulated evidence is selected. After each environment step, the reward history for the chosen action is updated via exponential moving average. This formulation directly instantiates postulates P1 (continuous evidence accumulation to threshold) and P2 (drift rate reflects stimulus quality) from Ratcliff (1978) and Bogacz et al. (2006).

variables.name: evidence_accumulator
variables.description: Running total of accumulated evidence favoring action i at internal time-step t. One accumulator per candidate action (6 total). Initialized at z0 each decision cycle.

variables.name: drift_rate
variables.description: Mean rate of evidence accumulation for action i, computed from perceptual proximity signal, eat-resource indicator, and reward history.

variables.name: noise_sample
variables.description: Independent Gaussian noise sample N(0,1) drawn at each internal integration step for each accumulator.

variables.name: chosen_action
variables.description: The action whose accumulator first crosses threshold a, or the argmax accumulator at timeout.

variables.name: decision_time
variables.description: Number of internal integration steps until a boundary crossing occurs (or T_max if timeout).

variables.name: reward_history
variables.description: Exponentially-weighted running mean of past rewards received after taking action i. One value per action (6 total).

parameters.name: decision_boundary
parameters.source: Ratcliff & McKoon (2008); typical range [0.5, 2.0] for moderate caution

parameters.name: integration_timestep
parameters.source: Discretization of Wiener process; Bogacz et al. (2006)

parameters.name: max_deliberation_steps
parameters.source: Prevents indefinite accumulation; practical computational bound

parameters.name: resource_proximity_scaling
parameters.source: Derived from postulate P2: stimulus quality maps to drift rate (Ratcliff, 1978)

parameters.name: eat_action_base_drift
parameters.source: Baseline desirability of eating when resource is present; linked to P2

parameters.name: reward_history_decay
parameters.source: Standard exponential moving average decay; knowledge backbone default

rules.description: Drift rate computation: combines resource proximity signal, eat-resource indicator, and reward history into a per-action drift rate.
rules.pseudocode:
for each action i in [move_up, move_down, move_left, move_right, stay, eat]:
  prox_i = 1.0 / (1.0 + manhattan_distance_to_nearest_food_in_direction(i))  # 0 if none visible
  eat_bonus = k_eat if (i == 'eat' and food_on_current_cell) else 0.0
  v_i = k_res * prox_i + eat_bonus + r_bar_i

rules.description: Evidence accumulation via discrete Wiener process: each accumulator evolves independently with drift and Gaussian noise.
rules.pseudocode:
X_i(t+1) = X_i(t) + v_i * dt + sigma * sqrt(dt) * random_normal(0, 1)
X_i(t+1) = clamp(X_i(t+1), 0, a)  # reflecting lower boundary at 0, absorbing upper at a

rules.description: Boundary-crossing decision: the first accumulator to reach or exceed the threshold a determines the chosen action.
rules.pseudocode:
for t in range(1, T_max + 1):
  update all X_i via R2
  winners = [i for i in actions if X_i(t) >= a]
  if winners:
    R = random_choice(winners)  # tie-breaking
    T_d = t
    break

rules.description: Timeout fallback: if no accumulator crosses the boundary within T_max steps, choose the action with the highest accumulator value.
rules.pseudocode:
if T_d is None:  # no boundary crossing
  R = argmax_i(X_i(T_max))
  T_d = T_max

rules.description: Reward history update: after observing reward, update the exponentially-weighted reward mean for the chosen action. Non-chosen actions decay toward zero.

decision_logic.description: Per-step decision: compute drift rates from perception, run a stochastic race among 6 accumulators (one per action), and return the winning action. After receiving reward feedback, update reward history traces.
pseudocode: food_on_cell = any(f['x'] == pos[0] and f['y'] == pos[1] for f in food_list)
# Step 2: Compute drift rates for each action via R1
d_i = min Manhattan distance from target to any food (or large number if no food)
prox_i = 0.0  # eat uses k_eat bonus instead
eat_bonus = k_eat if (i == 'eat' and food_on_cell) else 0.0
X = {i: z0 for i in actions}
X[i] = X[i] + v_i * dt + sigma * sqrt(dt) * random_normal()
winners = [i for i in actions if X[i] >= a]
chosen = argmax over actions of X[i]
# Step 7 (in update method): Update reward history via R5
for i != chosen: r_bar[i] = gamma * r_bar[i]

perception_to_variables.food_on_cell: any(f['x'] == x and f['y'] == y for f in perception['resources']['food'])

env_mapping.reward_source: eat action → ConsumeEffect with reward 1.0; move/stay actions yield reward 0.0

expected_behaviors.description: Agent navigates toward nearest food: drift rates for movement actions pointing toward food should dominate, causing those accumulators to win the race most often.
expected_behaviors.test_pseudocode: place food at (5,5), agent at (0,0) → over 50 steps, assert agent's mean distance to food decreases

expected_behaviors.description: Agent eats when on food: when food is present on the agent's cell, the eat accumulator receives a strong k_eat drift bonus, making it likely to win the race.
expected_behaviors.test_pseudocode: place agent on food cell → run decide() 100 times → assert eat is chosen > 60% of the time

expected_behaviors.description: Stochastic exploration: Gaussian noise in accumulators causes occasional non-optimal action choices, providing natural exploration without epsilon-greedy.
expected_behaviors.test_pseudocode: place agent equidistant from two food sources → run 100 decisions → assert both directions are chosen at least 20% each

expected_behaviors.description: Reward history biases future decisions: after repeatedly receiving reward from eating, the eat action's reward trace increases, biasing the accumulator.
expected_behaviors.test_pseudocode: give agent 10 eat rewards → assert r_bar['eat'] > r_bar['move_up']

expected_behaviors.description: Decision time varies with evidence quality: when one action has a much higher drift rate, the race resolves in fewer steps (lower T_d).
expected_behaviors.test_pseudocode: food directly adjacent (high prox) → mean T_d < T_d when food is far away

references: Ratcliff, R. (1978). A theory of memory retrieval. Psychological Review, 85(2), 59-108. DOI: 10.1037/0033-295X.85.2.59
Ratcliff, R., & Rouder, J. N. (1998). Modeling response times for two-choice decisions. Psychological Science, 9(5), 347-356. DOI: 10.1111/1467-9280.00067
Ratcliff, R., & McKoon, G. (2008). The diffusion decision model: Theory and data for two-choice decision tasks. Neural Computation, 20(4), 873-922. DOI: 10.1162/neco.2008.12-06-420
Bogacz, R., Brown, E., Moehlis, J., Holmes, P., & Cohen, J. D. (2006). The physics of optimal decision making. Psychological Review, 113(4), 700-765. DOI: 10.1037/0033-295X.113.4.700
